Hey, it's Sean and Andre. We are doing our first Facebook ad review for the Momentum Builder workshop in the in addition. First time we've done this, so we have no idea what it's actually going to look like. We'll talk about the structure first and how we're going to approach it and then recognize that no plan survives contact with reality, so we have no idea where we're going to end up. We've each chosen two ads as part of the review, this is really going to be thinking out loud. We have the copy open in front of us, we're going to go back and forth with some questions and we're just going to see where it leads. So a few things before we get started. One, there are five questions that we're using for ad review. You'll hear us read through the questions, we're adding these to module two, so you'll be able to go in and see these and do your own assessment. I mean, we highly, highly, highly recommend that you do that. These questions that we're using are the questions that you should use. So go look at your own ad or get a look at somebody else's if you want to do buddy stuff with each other, whatever it is, but use these questions and be honest. This is not, this is not when we say a critique, this isn't meant to, we're not trying to hurt anybody's feelings, we're not, this is not trying to be mean in any way. This is not emotional. And please don't take any feedback from each other or yourselves or anything else. Don't emotionalize it for yourselves because then all kinds of bad stuff happens. Just recognize that this is always an effort to make things better. And our feedback is going to be in service to that. And we've chosen ads that we think are broadly useful because there's something all of us can learn from them. One last comment before we get into the question. This is really feedback about all of the ads we've seen so far. I'm not sure there are any that don't fall into this category. The one thing I've certainly noticed more than anything else is that most of what you've created for hooks are not hooks. And to be clear, that is incredibly common. That's true for really high level copywriters. Often we have to write our way to finding a hook. And that process usually looks like, and many of you have done this very well, just to be clear, you sit down, you write, you write start to finish, or you just get it out of your head, a couple of drafts, and you've written the bait, what I would consider a base ad. At that point, when you're reading through the ad, somewhere in there, most likely two or three paragraphs down is the actual hook. You'll pop out, you'll see it, it grabs your attention. And I don't know why this is true. This is true for so much writing. We have to kind of write our way to a hook. At that point, you want to then, when you find it, you want to put that up at the top, you want to read, and then you want to reorient the ad in the writing around that new hook. It sounds like a lot of extra work, but it's, it's not like it's a little bit better, it's infinitely better once you find a really good hook. So, all right, here are the five questions. And the format is going to be, we'll read the question. And then we're each going, we're going to go back and forth, asking that question of the ads that we've chosen. So we can work our way through them. It'll take, I don't know, it'll take as long as it takes, I guess. All right, here are the five Facebook ad review questions. Question number one, do the first two sentences grab the prospect's attention to make him or her want to read more? The key word in there is grab. We have two sentences to really capture. You just like really arrest somebody's attention. Doesn't do that, be honest. Question number two, does a copy communicate empathy and understanding by focusing first on the prospect's needs, desires, fears and concerns. Question number three, is there a rising and falling of tension that pulls the reader's attention through the ad from beginning to end? Question number four, are the benefits from engaging with the writing obvious to the reader? And then finally, question number five, is the call to action interesting, compelling, and intriguing? All right, so I am going to ask the first question, and then I'm going to have Andre mentioned the ad that he's talking about, and then he's going to answer that question from his perspective on that ad, and then I'll go to one, and then we'll just go back and forth. All right, here's the question again. Do the first two sentences grab the prospect's attention and make him or her want to read more? Andre, which ad are you working on, and then take it away? All right, so let's do, I'm going to do Shane, Shane Mellinson, I'm sorry, I'm sorry if I'm butchering your surname, but it's Shane's ad, so it's about property investment. I don't know anything about property investment, but this is the ad I chose, because it had a story that seemed like it had the ingredients there, but okay, so we're going to be doing the hook. Shane's first sentence, or sentences started off as growing up in white court, I was taught to work hard, save your money, and one day eventually at 65, I could retire, and then it goes on, I didn't know the investment options, private versus public bonds, companies, and then it just kind of continues, and none of that grabbed my attention for a few reasons. One of which is if I had to just read that first sentence, it doesn't really identify me as who the target market is, so that, let's say, would have shown up in my feed. It's not calling out to me, it's just the start of someone's story, it needs to be a bit more obvious than that. So yeah, I've got a few suggestions, I'm sure it's not a great suggestion, it's not great, but I do want to mention that this is kind of broadly useful, that some hooks can be subtle and implied, and some can be avert and explicit, and I think the avert and explicit versions is what you see most of the times, and in fact, Sean, you commented on Shane's hook in the comments, so he said this, something like, and then you've got to invert the comments, these three mistakes cost new investors the most money, close, and so that's obviously calling out who the target market is, and then in brackets you've got, if I'm a new investor or thinking about becoming one, you can bet I want to know what those three mistakes are, and then you close, so that's far better, it's more avert, and yeah, I think that's the start of what I'm riffing on, the version that I've kind of gone with more just to demonstrate the other side of the coin, I guess, is a more subtle and implied story hook, and so I've got you, to the uninitiated, every property deal presents, every property deal presented looks great, and for my family, our first deal looked gold plated, but there are hard lessons in this business, and at 19 I learned mine, and my father lost $100,000 of his savings, so that's kind of one long, well, two sentences over three lines, I'm not quite sure what, when Facebook truncates the, but that can show up in the first sentence, in the first line, I don't know what your thoughts are on that, Sean, but... We're going to be good, not bad, comp, except for implicit Andre versus explicit Sean, yeah, one of the things, as I looked through it, I thought there was possibility to how my family lost it all, so you're coming out of sort of no bleak angle, and it's, my tendency is to just come in with a slight hammer, so if I were taking that same hook, I would probably emphasize something like how my family lost it all when I was 19, and what you can learn from it, or I wouldn't use you, obviously, but I tend to be far more explicit in the hook, just because I don't want somebody to scroll past it, and that's going to show up as we, actually, I'll transition to mine now, if you're good with the hook on this one, and we'll really see the good comp, bad comp, explicit comp, implicit comp, because I'm just, I'm looking for the most explicit hooks possible, all right, so we'll jump over. Let me just ask one question, I'm going to play devil's advocate, see you the Facebook expert, yeah, and I'm not, presumably, if somebody has maybe two versions of the hook, so the core story, so the ads, let's say, is the same, but those first two sentences are different, presumably it's easy enough just to run them both, and then allow the market to kind of dictate which one is pulling in the right people, yeah, and that's, I mean, the thing that we test the most, or that's not, yeah, I think we probably tested the most, but it's certainly the most powerful thing to test is a hook, and the thing, the other thing, I don't want to go into a deep dive on the Facebook idiosyncrasies, but another thing that I recognize too, is in very large audiences, different hooks will attract different people, so it makes perfect sense, and this is what, this is one of the things that makes A.B. testing on Facebook so difficult too, it's this idea that you can never step into the same stream twice, and you've got context changes, the news is changing every day, what's going on in people's feeds changes every moment to moment, and so when you A.B. test, you're catching different people, and you're also catching them at different times in different contexts, so the answer is to your question is always test, and test hook versus hook is a great place to test, and if you have, if you have smaller budgets, just A.B. test hooks, don't try to test five hooks, just test two, and in a good place to start actually, we didn't plan on this, but this is an interesting side note, a good place to start is explicit versus implicit, right, do like a subtle ramp up kind of memoir style, if that makes sense, and then do that relative, just versus a very explicit call out, who's the audience, what's the big benefit, that would be a great one to test, that's a great question. Cool, all right, you ready to move on to the next one? Okay. We're gonna be here all day, oh lord. imposter was actually a superpower, so that's interesting to me, so I would, I would probably move, I would probably make that a hook, the challenge with that is it doesn't call out your audience, which is women in business, so you've got to, you've got to merge those two things together somehow, so, but that, that to me is interesting, is if I, if I'm a woman in business, and I'm dealing with imposter syndrome, you've now introduced something that is counterintuitive, and with your hook you've set the frame, and it's a really interesting frame, just think about how we imagine imposter syndrome in general, the, we think about imposter, and for everyone who's not aware of what imposter syndrome is, very quick, you, very quick high-level explanation, is imposter syndrome is when you, you feel like at any moment you're about to be found out that you're not, you know, nearly as good as people think you are, or whatever else, it's common, particularly among high achievers that we think at any moment, I just did a thing that I didn't mean to do, I put myself in a category of high achievers, I had used the imperial we, but I suffer from imposter syndrome, I mean I wake up every morning thinking, today's the day when everyone's going to find out that I don't actually know anything, whatever, that's imposter syndrome, so conventional wisdom about imposter syndrome is that it's a bad thing, right, like with something you suffer from, you need to, the hook even implies you need to beat imposter syndrome, but if you, so that's the frame, like we've sort of set this frame that what if, you know, what if, you know, if you could, the frame or the hook here is, is suggesting that we need to beat imposter syndrome, but then later, what I, we say, what if it's actually a superpower, well you wouldn't want to beat a superpower, you'd want to, you'd want to amplify a superpower, so I would move that up, I would make that, I would make that the hook and frame everything that follows in this ad around this idea that what you think is true about imposter syndrome, that it's something you have to overcome, is actually not true at all, imposter syndrome can be fuel and it can, it's a superpower that actually can drive high, high achievers, that's where I think the hook is here. Yeah, Andre, what's your next one? Oh yeah, you want to riff on this one too? Yeah, we can riff on each other too, that'll be fun. Yeah, I know, it's just, I just wanted to draw attention to the fact that, that, that whole idea that attaching superpower to, to the negative trait is, is very intriguing. I would probably mention the fuel part further down, I've not read all of this one, so I don't know at which part it's mentioned where the fuel can be, you know, I think I made that up actually. Okay, all right. Okay, in that case, all good then. Yeah, I like that a lot. And I think that's, and something you just mentioned, something you just mentioned is interesting, broadly speaking, when you, when you turn, when you reframe something that's thought to be or presented as a negative into a positive, my daughter is dyslexic, and one of the things we've always done with her is talk, is we've done that, like conventional wisdom is like, oh, dyslexia is a learning disability, and what conversations I've had with her, like, well, that's curious, because, you know, Einstein was dyslexic. So, and if you look through the, the, if you look through the historical record, the recent historical record of people who are dyslexic, you see unbelievable height achievers, and it's, in many ways, you could use the same format, dyslexia is a superpower. So, we can always turn things around, we can all, and again, this is a, this wasn't intentional, this is a perfect example of what framing is. The conventional frame is that something is X, something is negative, or has negative consequences, and you take that conventional wisdom, and you reframe it, and you say, well, this way you've been thinking about things, and the way we've been led to think about things is actually 180 degrees wrong. It's, if you look at it this way, you can't know what can seem to be like turning my hand as I'm doing, if you look at it this way instead, you'll realize that it's actually, and then fill in the blank. That's a very compelling hook, a very compelling way to reframe, immediately to reframe. All right, I'm going to hear my own voice. I mean, my concern was stones is there, from a philosophical standpoint, Facebook does not like when we, when advertisers try to get around a stated rule. Right. So if there's, like, if there's a note, one of Facebook's rules is don't use profanity in your ads. So if somebody tries to do like the abbreviated, you know, with the symbols, where a leader knows it's profanity, like Facebook looks at that and sees that as an attempt to undermine its, its terms of service. Right. Like, and it's, you know, and you might look and say, that's all in good fun or whatever. But as a policy, Facebook, that seems to be one of the philosophical things that guides Facebook decision making where it's like, Hey, we have this policy and you've done this thing trying to be cute to get around it. And that, for whatever reason, just pisses off Facebook. And I've actually seen that lead to account shutdowns where it's, it's, and it's, you know, it's not meant, it's not meant to be, I think the Brits would call it, you know, you're trying to be cheeky for Nick here, but Facebook doesn't, Facebook doesn't do cheeky. Right. There's no like, Hey, that was cute. It's more like, Hey, here's a sledgehammer. We'll hit you with it and don't do it again. So it's very similar in principle to if you have an account shut down and you just spin up a brand new account and start all over again, like Facebook sees that as, or if you have an account like suspended, and then, and then you just create a new ad account, like, Oh, whatever, I'm just not going to deal with that other account. I'll just create a new ad account. Like to Facebook, that's you trying to get around whatever caused your account to be shut down. They just don't like that, like trying to get around their rules. So I don't, I just wouldn't do that. I wouldn't take the chance for that one. And I don't think it's the hook anyway. So it's kind of a move point. Yeah. Okay. From my perspective, it's great. I think, you know, one of the core tenets of a well-told story, a good story is that it elicits emotion. That's like the key ingredient. And at the same time, there's quite a big difference between showing versus telling. And I think she's told us a lot of things that demonstrate that she's, you know, there's understanding for her audience. But I think it can be a lot more powerful by telling somebody else's story and for, you know, and to suck readers in into a story. And through reading that story, then the reader, you know, feels the emotion as opposed to just hearing somebody say something that demonstrates that they understand something. You know, it's subtle. But when you read a story that does the other version, it's very, very powerful. So I think that's my new point. There's a lot here. I mean, if you're a woman with imposter syndrome, you're just drawn in immediately. They're getting, they, this is the, this is the counterintuitive frame that, that doesn't make sense to this audience, which is they're doing something to help people yet they're hurting the person who's most important to them in the process. That's the conflict here. Right. They're having to cancel date night with their spouse, the person who they've, they've, is arguably the most important person in their life to help other people, most of whom are strangers. Right. So, and they've done, and they've done all this other stuff to help, because they want so desperately to help other people that they're ruining their relationship in the process, or they're undermining their relationship with the process or whatever it is, they're hurting themselves and hurting the people closest to them in the service to helping others. Like that's, that's a powerful frame to set. It's counterintuitive. What you think you're doing is to be helpful, but what is actually happening is you're hurting the people closest to you. That's a very powerful frame. And later in the, when we talked about it, we can get to the end of the ad, toward the end of the ad, that frame reverses and shows that there is a way where you can actually help other people even more, even more intensely, and do that without hurting the people around you. That's a really cool frame. Now you've, you've, you've really taken this thing. You've, you've, you're shy, Francis could shine light on something that's true that they don't, that they haven't connected the dots for, which is their desire to help other people is hurting the people closest to them. But then he can reframe it and say, but there is a way to help the people, to help people to do that thing that you want to do and to actually help them more while also maintaining the things that are, the relationships that are most important to you. There is a rising and falling here, more rising. And that's hard. The falling part is hard. We talked about this yesterday, which is, and I drew it on the board behind my chair, which when I think about rising and falling tension, I'm imagining one line is sort of a long progression of gently climbing. And if you can, so, you know, we made the thematic arc as an arc now and think maybe it should have been a line that's taking somebody from lower to higher or whatever. But if you can imagine that line, is the baseline, like that's the thing that if you walk the narrowest path, you're going from A to B from unknown to purchase or unknown to whatever, to signing up for a free quote, whatever, or something you're validating, whatever it is, that's the long line of the thematic arc. And then the ads and the landing page and everything else are like this sort of oscillating line that are rising above it and then coming back down to it and rising and falling. There's like a rising and falling of tension. There are opening loops and closing loops. But the big loop, the big thing, the what's it all about is not closed until the end. And if you think about reading fiction, you know, every scene pulls you forward, every chapter pulls you forward, but it's all pulling you forward in service of the big, the hero's journey, the big thing, the big theme of the book that doesn't finally resolve until the end. And then we all be back like, ah, right, that's it. That's a good book. And we're not writing a book, but we're writing I don't know, 20% of one ish in length. But we need to do the exact same thing. We need to rise and fall and rise and fall. But the rising and falling is on a line that's rising. We're bringing this all to a conclusion, which is there's something that they can get that's going to change their lives in whatever it is relative to what it is that you sell. So with that as context, the Facebook ad needs to rise and fall as well. And we're much better at the rising. Is there, you know, rising and falling tension. Let's get, I'll give you one example, which I think is a good example of, of tension that's, it's sort of plateaued and tension introduced. So this is pretty far down the ad copy. So it was, it was obviously, especially when we saw the kind of art and artists that did sell, that were making money often in the scene. So unfair at the time, it wasn't about the work, the talent or the tenacity. It was something else that the, it was something else phrase is a perfect example of rising tension. Because there's sort of this progression. You know, there's, there's observation, there's observation. He's telling me something. He's telling me what it's wasn't about this, wasn't about that, wasn't even about this, was something else. Now I'm like, what the hell was it? Right. And then, but he, and this is actually, this, this is a really, when I say trick, I don't mean it, like a negative hacks, I mean, it's a good writing trick. So he says it was something else. Now I'm curious, something simple, but immensely powerful. Oh, son of a bitch, come on, man, like, now the tension is really rising. And then he resolves it. Those artists knew how to tell a story. Oh, okay. Like that's, that's tension, right? That's, that's a, those three sentences are a really good example for sentences are really good examples of at the micro level, the rising and falling of tension. I suspect because Nick isn't very talented writer, he's doing this unconscious, it's unconscious competence. But that's a really beautiful example of how to build tension, but like a little peak of tension, not quite resolved the tension, which amplifies the peak that you just created, and then resolve it. And, and now, even though we resolved it, there's still tension because I'm like, they know how to tell a story. Oh, interesting. But I don't know, like, now I want to know, what does he mean? Are they not a tell a story? Like, that's, yeah, this, that's a beautiful example within this ad of just really talented writing. There's also this, there's some parts that he's, that he uses language in a very clever way. For my wife, it was like watching a foreign language film, you know, as you read that, you straight away, you can feel what that's like, right? It's, it's, you, you get the, get the feeling of what it's like watching a movie where you, where people are talking and you don't understand a single word. And he's said it in just three words or two words. It's very clever. Yeah, I, it's funny. That's a spiky part for me, though, and I would change it to, I would, and again, this is idiotic. So just, it's one, it's one person's opinion. I'm just, I'm, I'm hyper sensitive to a person describing another person's experience. I don't know why I'm so overly sensitive to that. I think the way I would have written that, which again, is idiotic to even say it like this, because this is, this is Nick's life and story and expertise, but I would approach that from, instead of saying for my wife, it was like watching a foreign language film, I would say, I would change that to dialogue or yo, yo, yo, or I would change it to the message being more like, my wife described it as like watching a foreign language film, where she's taking the active role, Nick's not describing what he thinks it was like. And maybe that doesn't fit. And maybe it's hypersensitivity. I just, I don't, I'm just, I'm, I'm, I'm terribly cautious about describing the experience of another person. I don't know, maybe it works here. I don't know, but that, that, that to me, there's, and I think that's just a lesson. There are lots of ways to say the same thing. One of the questions you need to ask, and you know, any kind of writing is who's doing the talking here? You know, am I, am I doing the talking? Am I always doing the talking as a narrator, or am I, am I an observing narrator where I am also a witness to some of the things that are being said? Like, how am I communicating? And it's, it's more like, this is more, we get into what, his wife saying something, we're getting more into screenwriting principles where you're, you're stating the action, not describing the action that you've observed. So, and I think there tends to be a little more evocative emotional power. And if you don't like outlines, longest time I had terrible difficulty outlining first. But another way you can do this is you can you can write and then come back and create the outline of what you wrote. And then look at that outline and then reorder the outline so it flows better.
